children.name: Rallus
children.description: Water rails — secretive, laterally compressed marsh birds built to slip silently through dense reedbed. The water rail (vattenrall) is far more often heard than seen: its extraordinary calls range from a squealing piglet-like shriek to a rhythmic grunting that echoes across Swedish marshes on still evenings. Despite their skulking habits, water rails are present in virtually every Swedish reedbed of any size, from the Baltic coast to inland lakes around Stockholm. Their laterally flattened bodies — the origin of the expression 'thin as a rail' — let them move at speed through tangled reed stems inaccessible to larger predators. In hard winters when Swedish reedbeds freeze over, water rails sometimes starve; in milder years they are sedentary and can be found year-round.

children.name: Rallus aquaticus
children.description: The vattenrall — one of Sweden's most heard but rarely seen birds. Its pig-like squealing calls ('sharming') carry far across reedbed at dawn and dusk. Found in reedbeds across Sweden from Skåne to Uppland; sedentary where wetlands remain ice-free in winter. Its long red bill probes soft mud for invertebrates, small fish, and plant material. A patient observer sitting quietly at a reedbed edge may eventually watch one emerge to feed at the margin.

children.name: Rallus elegans
children.description: The largest North American rail, occupying freshwater and brackish marshes from the Great Lakes south to the Gulf Coast. Its chestnut breast and heavily barred flanks make it a striking bird when glimpsed through reeds. The King Rail is a Species of Conservation Concern in much of its range due to widespread wetland drainage; interior populations have declined significantly since the mid-20th century.

children.name: Rallus obsoletus
children.description: A large, secretive rail of Pacific coastal saltmarshes from California south to Baja California and mainland Mexico. Once treated as conspecific with Clapper Rail, Ridgway's Rail was split following molecular analysis that revealed deep genetic divergence. Several subspecies are Critically Endangered due to habitat loss from urban encroachment on tidal marshes, and active conservation programmes manage nest sites and control invasive cordgrass.

children.name: Rallus longirostris
children.description: A long-billed rail endemic to mangrove forests and tidal mudflats along the Atlantic and Pacific coasts of South America. Its narrow body allows it to navigate dense prop-root tangles largely inaccessible to other birds. Until recently lumped with Clapper Rail in a broad complex; now recognised as a distinct species with its own distinctive call and plumage tones.

children.name: Rallus crepitans
children.description: An abundant rail of Atlantic and Gulf Coast saltmarshes, whose loud, clattering 'kek-kek-kek' calls are a signature sound of eastern North American tidal wetlands. Highly adapted to saltmarsh life, nesting above mean high tide and foraging for fiddler crabs, molluscs, and small fish. Populations fluctuate with storm events and sea-level rise, making coastal conservation crucial.

children.name: Rallus limicola
children.description: A small, richly coloured rail of freshwater and brackish marshes across North America, wintering south to Central America and coastal South America. Despite its secretive nature, the Virginia Rail's descending 'kidick' call is a reliable indicator of its presence in cattail and sedge wetlands. Unusually for a rail, it regularly undertakes long nocturnal migrations, and vagrant individuals occasionally appear far outside normal range.

children.name: Rallus longirostris
children.description: A rail of coastal mangroves from Panama to Brazil. Its long, down-curved bill is adapted for probing mud in the mangal ecosystem.

children.name: Rallus limicola
children.description: A small, chestnut-brown rail of North American freshwater marshes. More often heard than seen — its sharp 'kid-ick, kid-ick' call is a characteristic sound of reedbeds across the United States and Canada.

children.name: Rallus semiplumbeus
children.description: A critically endangered rail endemic to the high-altitude wetlands of the Eastern Andes in Colombia. Threatened by the destruction of its specialised wetland habitat around Bogotá.

children.name: Rallus aquaticus
children.description: A secretive, elusive rail of European and Asian reedbeds. Far more often heard than seen — its extraordinary vocal repertoire includes pig-like squeals, yelps, and a distinctive 'tyick-tyick-tyick' call that carries across marshes on still evenings. Its long red bill probes deep into soft mud for insects and worms. In Sweden, the vattenrall is a classic bird of the Skåne reedbeds, its spring song, a rhythmic 'kut-kut-kut' often incorrectly described as sounding like a piglet, a motif well known to generations of Nordic birders who track the species by ear through the impenetrable reedbeds of Lake Tåkern and Ottenby.

children.name: Rallus antarcticus
children.description: A small, dark rail of southern Argentina and Chile, found in marshes from sea level to the Andean foothills. Its population declined dramatically due to habitat loss and introduced predators.

children.name: Rallus wetmorei
children.description: A critically endangered rail of the Venezuelan Caribbean coast, confined to a few remaining mangrove patches. Named after the American ornithologist Alexander Wetmore.

children.name: Rallus caerulescens
children.description: A handsome blue-grey rail of eastern and southern African marshes. Its bright red bill and legs contrast with the slate-blue body plumage.

children.name: Rallus madagascariensis
children.description: A rail endemic to the marshes of eastern Madagascar. Threatened by habitat destruction and the introduction of predatory fish to the island's remaining wetlands.

children.name: Lewinia
children.description: Lewin's rails — slender Indo-Pacific rails closely related to Rallus but distinguished by molecular phylogeny and subtle morphological differences. Named after the Australian naturalist John William Lewin, this small genus occupies dense grasslands and sedge wetlands from Australia through Southeast Asia. Species are typically more tolerant of drier habitats than true water rails.

children.name: Lewinia pectoralis
children.description: A richly patterned rail of wet grasslands, sedge swamps, and coastal heathlands in eastern and south-western Australia. Its chestnut face and neck contrast with grey underparts and heavily streaked brown upperparts. Although widespread, Lewin's Rail is rarely seen due to its preference for dense, wet vegetation, and it is most reliably detected by its harsh, repetitive call.

children.name: Lewinia striata
children.description: A widespread rail of freshwater marshes, rice paddies, and mangrove edges from South Asia through Southeast Asia to the Philippines and northern Australia. Its slate-grey breast and chestnut head make it the most colourful member of the genus. Tolerant of disturbed habitats including irrigated agriculture, it is locally common despite its secretive behaviour.

children.name: Gallirallus
children.description: Forest and island rails — a predominantly Indo-Pacific genus remarkable for repeated evolution of flightlessness on oceanic islands. The genus includes some of the world's most iconic examples of island endemism and extinction: dozens of species have been lost since human settlement of the Pacific, making the surviving members priority conservation targets. Gallirallus rails are typically more terrestrial than water-dependent relatives, occupying forest floors, grasslands, and scrub.

children.name: Gallirallus australis
children.description: A large, flightless rail endemic to New Zealand, where it inhabits coastal scrub, forest margins, and offshore islands. The Weka is an opportunistic omnivore with a bold, inquisitive temperament, famously stealing food and small objects from campers. Depleted by introduced predators on the main islands, populations have been partially restored through island translocations and pest control programmes.

children.name: Gallirallus philippensis
children.description: One of the most widespread and variable rails in the world, with subspecies distributed from the Philippines and Wallacea through New Guinea, Australia, and across the Pacific to Fiji and Samoa. Its buff-orange breast band and boldly barred underparts are diagnostic. The Buff-banded Rail's broad distribution reflects a propensity to colonise new islands; some populations have evolved reduced flight in the absence of predators.

children.name: Gallirallus torquatus
children.description: A handsome, boldly barred rail of dense grasslands, forest edges, and secondary growth in the Philippines, Sulawesi, and the Moluccas. Its strong bill and long toes are adapted for foraging in tangled ground vegetation for invertebrates and seeds. The Barred Rail is heard more often than seen, producing a loud, repetitive series of notes particularly at dawn and dusk.

children.name: Gallirallus sylvestris
children.description: A Critically Endangered flightless rail surviving only on Lord Howe Island in the Tasman Sea, where the total population numbers fewer than 50 individuals. Intensive conservation management, including predator eradication and captive breeding, has prevented extinction but the species remains precarious. The Lord Howe Rail is a flagship species for island conservation and a sobering reminder of the vulnerability of flightless island birds.

children.name: Aramides
children.description: Wood-rails — large, long-legged Neotropical rails of forested wetlands, mangroves, and dense riparian vegetation. Distinguishable from most rallids by their substantial size, boldly patterned plumage combining grey, chestnut, and olive tones, and their loud, often raucous calls that carry through lowland forest. Wood-rails are more often heard at dawn and dusk than seen, but are bolder than many family members when venturing to forest edges.

children.name: Aramides cajaneus
children.description: The most widespread wood-rail, occurring from Mexico through Central America to northern Argentina in forested wetlands, mangroves, and gallery forest. Its grey neck and head contrasting with chestnut breast and olive-brown back make it one of the most distinctive Neotropical rallids. Like other wood-rails, it produces loud, cackling duets at dawn and dusk that are characteristic sounds of lowland rainforest.

children.name: Aramides calopterus
children.description: A little-known wood-rail restricted to humid foothill forests in the western Amazon basin of Ecuador and Peru. It is distinguished from related species by its rich rufous-chestnut wing coverts and is one of the most poorly documented rallids in South America. Its ecology and population status are largely unknown due to the difficulty of accessing its dense forest habitat.

children.name: Aramides saracura
children.description: An Atlantic Forest endemic restricted to south-eastern Brazil, Paraguay, and north-eastern Argentina, where it occupies humid lowland forest near watercourses and swamps. Its grey-blue breast sharply demarcated from rufous flanks distinguishes it from sympatric wood-rails. Although associated with threatened Atlantic Forest habitat, the Slaty-breasted Wood-Rail tolerates some forest degradation and remains locally common.

children.name: Aramides ypecaha
children.description: The largest species in the genus, approaching the size of a small chicken, found in marshes and wet grasslands of the Pampas region in south-eastern South America. Unlike most wood-rails, the Giant Wood-Rail regularly inhabits open wetlands and is less dependent on forest cover. Its large size and bold habits make it more conspicuous than its congeners, and it is frequently heard calling from marshes in Argentina, Uruguay, and southern Brazil.

children.name: Crex
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Corncrake, a migratory rallid unique among European rails for breeding in dry meadows rather than wetlands. The genus name derives from the Greek for the bird's rasping call. Despite its terrestrial habits, the Corncrake undertakes long migrations between European breeding grounds and African wintering areas, travelling largely on foot through grass cover and flying only when necessary.

children.name: Crex crex
children.description: A migratory rallid of hay meadows and tall grasslands, whose penetrating 'krek-krek' call has become increasingly rare across much of Europe following the mechanisation of agriculture. The Corncrake breeds in Eurasia and winters in sub-Saharan Africa, an extraordinary journey for a bird that prefers to walk rather than fly. Conservation measures including delayed mowing of hay meadows have stabilised populations in some areas, but it remains of global conservation concern.

children.name: Amaurornis
children.description: Bush-hens and waterhen — a genus of medium-sized rallids widely distributed across Asia and the Pacific, typically occupying dense vegetation near wetlands, rice paddies, and mangrove edges. Several species are among the most adaptable and urban-tolerant rallids, thriving in degraded habitats and suburban gardens near water. The White-breasted Waterhen is among the most frequently observed Asian rails.

children.name: Amaurornis phoenicurus
children.description: One of the most common and visible rallids in Asia, found from India and Sri Lanka through Southeast Asia to southern China and the Philippines. Its bold white face and breast against dark upperparts are distinctive, and unlike many rails it frequently walks in the open along paths, roadsides, and garden ponds. The White-breasted Waterhen is highly adaptable, thriving in urban parks, rice paddies, and mangrove creeks alike.

children.name: Amaurornis akool
children.description: A plain brown rallid of dense riverine and marsh vegetation from Pakistan through India to southern China and Southeast Asia. Less conspicuous than the White-breasted Waterhen, the Brown Crake is rarely seen in the open and relies on its uniform brown plumage for camouflage in reed and sedge stands. It is detected primarily by its distinctive calls — a series of loud, frog-like notes delivered at dusk.

children.name: Amaurornis olivaceus
children.description: A uniformly olive-brown rail of forest understory, dense scrub, and mangroves in the Philippines, New Guinea, and northern Australia. Its plain plumage with a rufous-buff wash on the undertail distinguishes it from sympatric species. The Plain Bush-hen is a shy inhabitant of dense vegetation and is most active at dawn and dusk, when its loud, repetitive calls betray its presence.

children.name: Amaurornis moluccanus
children.description: A distinctive rallid of mangroves, swamp forest, and tall grass on the Moluccas, Sulawesi, New Guinea, and northern Australia, recognisable by its bright rufous undertail coverts. Like other bush-hens it is secretive but vocal, especially at dusk. The Rufous-tailed Bush-hen has benefited from irrigated agricultural expansion in parts of its range, where rice paddies provide suitable foraging habitat.

children.name: Porzana
children.description: Spotted crakes — small to medium-sized rallids of dense freshwater marsh vegetation, historically comprising a broad assemblage now substantially revised by molecular phylogenetics. The Spotted Crake and Sora are among the most familiar representatives; both are migratory species that undertake extensive movements between breeding and wintering grounds. Porzana crakes are characterised by spotted or streaked brown and white patterning that provides excellent camouflage in reed and sedge habitat.

children.name: Porzana porzana
children.description: A secretive summer visitor to European marshes, wintering in sub-Saharan Africa. Its spotted white plumage on olive-brown upperparts and distinctive 'whiplash' call — a rhythmic 'hwuit' repeated at one-second intervals — are the best field marks. The Spotted Crake has declined across much of its European range due to wetland drainage, and it is now an uncommon breeder in many countries where it was once widespread.

children.name: Porzana carolina
children.description: The most abundant and widespread North American rallid, breeding in freshwater marshes across Canada and the northern United States and wintering from the Gulf Coast south to northern South America. Despite its secretive nature, the Sora's high-pitched whinny and descending 'kerwee' calls are familiar wetland sounds. In autumn, staging Soras can concentrate in tremendous numbers in Atlantic Coast tidal marshes, fattening on wild rice before southward migration.

children.name: Porzana porzana
children.description: A small, secretive rail of Swedish marshes and wet meadows. Its distinctive, sharp 'hwitt, hwitt' call — like the crack of a whip — is heard at night from May through July in reedbeds across Sweden. Spotted crakes are rarely seen, skulking in dense vegetation, but their distinctive call carries far across marshland at dusk. Favours sedge beds and seasonally flooded meadows with a mix of shallow water and tussocks.

children.name: Porzana carolina
children.description: The most common rail of North American marshes, familiar for its whinnying, descending call that is a staple sound of wetlands across the continent from the Everglades to the Prairie Pothole Region, where it is a common breeder in the vast cattail and bulrush stands that dot the Dakotas, Manitoba, and Saskatchewan. Its bright yellow bill and grey face with a black mask make it one of the more distinctive of the small rails.

children.name: Porzana flaviventer
children.description: A tiny crake with a yellow breast and white belly, found across the Caribbean and from Mexico to Argentina. Easily overlooked due to its size and secretive habits.

children.name: Porzana albicollis
children.description: A medium-sized crake of South American wetlands, with a grey throat and chest and brown upperparts. More often heard than seen — its loud, rhythmic call carries across marshes.

children.name: Porzana fusca
children.description: A small crake of marshes and rice paddies across South and East Asia. Its reddish-brown plumage and distinctive whinnying call make it the most familiar crake in much of its range.

children.name: Porzana paykullii
children.description: A striking crake of eastern China and Southeast Asia, with a prominent black-and-white barred belly. Named after the Swedish naturalist Gustaf von Paykull.

children.name: Porzana pusilla
children.description: The smallest of the crakes, barely larger than a sparrow. Its tiny size, cryptic plumage, and habit of creeping through dense vegetation make it one of the most difficult wetland birds to observe in the field. Widespread but extremely local across Scandinavia, it calls with a loud, rattling trill at dawn.

children.name: Porzana tabuensis
children.description: A uniformly dark, sooty-brown crake found from the Philippines to the Pacific islands. Its distribution across remote islands of the Pacific makes it one of the most widespread of the small crakes.

children.name: Zapornia
children.description: A genus of small to minute crakes resurrected from synonymy with Porzana following molecular revision. Zapornia includes some of the world's smallest rails as well as several island species, some of which are extinct or Critically Endangered. The genus spans the Old and New World tropics, with species occupying marshes, flooded grasslands, and wetland edges. Several Pacific island endemics have been lost to introduced predators since human settlement.

children.name: Zapornia pusilla
children.description: One of the world's smallest rallids, barely larger than a sparrow, with a vast distribution from Western Europe across Asia to Australia and sub-Saharan Africa. Its blue-grey underparts and finely barred flanks distinguish it from similar crakes. Baillon's Crake is exceptionally secretive and seldom observed despite being relatively widespread; it is more often caught by ornithologists ringing at reedbeds than recorded by birdwatchers.

children.name: Zapornia parva
children.description: A small, elegantly patterned crake breeding in reedbeds and flooded sedge marshes from Central Europe eastward through Central Asia, wintering in tropical Africa. The male's plain blue-grey underparts and olive-brown upperparts distinguish it from the similarly sized Baillon's Crake. The Little Crake has experienced local declines due to wetland drainage but remains locally numerous in its core breeding areas around the Black and Caspian Seas.

children.name: Zapornia tabuensis
children.description: A small, uniformly dark crake of freshwater and brackish wetlands distributed from the Philippines and Micronesia through New Guinea and Australia to New Zealand and eastern Polynesia. Its wholly dark plumage with red bill and legs distinguishes it from other small crakes. The Spotless Crake is more tolerant of disturbed habitat than many family members and can be found in suburban wetlands where dense reed or sedge cover remains.

children.name: Zapornia palmeri
children.description: An extinct flightless crake that once inhabited Laysan Island in the Hawaiian archipelago, last recorded in 1944 following the introduction of rabbits that destroyed the island's vegetation. Introduced to Midway Atoll as a conservation measure, the transplanted population was extirpated by introduced rats during World War II. The Laysan Rail stands as a tragic example of island rail vulnerability and the irreversibility of extinction.

children.name: Zapornia sandwichensis
children.description: An extinct flightless crake known from the island of Hawai'i, last collected in the 1880s and almost certainly extirpated by introduced rats and mongooses. Known only from a handful of museum specimens and early collector accounts, the Hawaiian Rail may have still survived in remote upland areas into the early 20th century. Like the Laysan Rail, it exemplifies the catastrophic impact of introduced predators on island rallids.

children.name: Laterallus
children.description: A genus of tiny to small Neotropical crakes occupying dense marsh vegetation, wet grasslands, and flooded fields from Mexico to southern South America, with one species ranging to the Caribbean. Laterallus rails are among the most secretive birds in the Americas; several species are known from only a handful of records, and one — the Bogotá Rail — was undiscovered until the early 20th century despite occurring near a major city. The genus includes several species of conservation concern due to wetland loss.

children.name: Laterallus jamaicensis
children.description: One of North America's most secretive and rarely seen birds, the size of a sparrow, inhabiting tidal saltmarshes and wet meadows along both coasts of North America. Its all-dark plumage with fine white spotting on the back and red eye make it almost impossible to observe in dense marsh grass. The Eastern Black Rail was listed as Threatened under the US Endangered Species Act in 2020 following steep population declines driven by coastal habitat loss and sea-level rise.

children.name: Laterallus albigularis
children.description: A small, richly coloured crake of wet lowland forests, swampy thickets, and riparian vegetation from Honduras through Central America to western Ecuador and Colombia. Its white throat and breast contrasting with chestnut flanks are distinctive. Although secretive, the White-throated Crake is not uncommon and can be found by patiently playing recordings of its territorial call in appropriate habitat.

children.name: Laterallus melanophaius
children.description: A small crake of wet grasslands, marsh edges, and rice paddies widely distributed across lowland South America east of the Andes. Its rufous flanks, barred with white, and grey head distinguish it from similar crakes. The Rufous-sided Crake is locally common in suitable habitat and is one of the more frequently recorded Laterallus species, occasionally venturing to damp roadsides where it can be briefly observed.

children.name: Laterallus exilis
children.description: A tiny, grey-breasted crake of humid lowland forest floor and dense ground vegetation from Central America through the Amazon basin. Its plain grey underparts and brown upperparts lack the distinctive markings of many family members, making it difficult to identify. The Grey-breasted Crake is one of the least-known Laterallus species and is encountered infrequently even by experienced field ornithologists working in its range.

children.name: Sarothrura
children.description: Flufftails — a genus of small, skulking African rails characterised by fluffy, fan-like tails and sexually dimorphic plumage in which males are brightly coloured and females cryptically streaked. The genus is endemic to sub-Saharan Africa and Madagascar. Flufftails are among the most poorly known birds in Africa: most species inhabit dense grass or wetland vegetation and are detected almost exclusively by their calls, which in several species are remarkable booming sounds produced by air sacs.

children.name: Sarothrura pulchra
children.description: The most widespread African flufftail, found in forest understory and dense thickets from West Africa through Central Africa to Uganda and Tanzania. The male's chestnut head and neck, blackish body, and white-spotted wings are striking in the brief moments it is glimpsed. Like most flufftails, the White-spotted Flufftail is detected mainly by its purring call, and population assessments rely on call surveys rather than visual detection.

children.name: Sarothrura elegans
children.description: A forest-floor flufftail of montane and riparian forest from Ethiopia south to South Africa, where it is detected by its distinctive booming call — a deep, resonant note repeated at intervals and produced with the aid of inflatable throat sacs. The Buff-spotted Flufftail is rarely observed directly due to its dense habitat and cryptic behaviour. It is among the most commonly heard but least often seen birds in East African forest birding.

children.name: Sarothrura rufa
children.description: A flufftail of dense wetland vegetation, flooded grasslands, and sedge marshes from Nigeria and Ethiopia south to South Africa. The male's rich chestnut breast and head are among the brightest colours of any African rallid. Its call — a descending, frog-like series of notes — is a characteristic sound of marshy grasslands at altitude, where the species reaches its greatest densities in East and Southern Africa.

children.name: Coturnicops
children.description: A small genus of tiny, sparrow-sized crakes with highly cryptic, heavily streaked plumage adapted for concealment in dense wetland vegetation. Species in this genus are among the most elusive of all North American and East Asian waterbirds, rarely seen despite being locally present in suitable habitat. They are closely associated with grassy freshwater wetlands and undertake nocturnal migrations.

children.name: Coturnicops noveboracensis
children.description: One of the most secretive birds in North America, inhabiting dense sedge and wet-grass meadows across the northern United States and Canada. Its yellow-buff plumage streaked with black and white offers near-perfect concealment in standing marsh grass; the species is most reliably detected by its repetitive 'ticking' call resembling two stones struck together. The Yellow Rail is rarely flushed and has been called the most difficult regular North American bird species to see well.

children.name: Gallinula
children.description: Moorhens — medium-sized rallids that occupy the interface between open water and emergent vegetation, making them far more visible than most family members. The common moorhen (rörhöna) is a ubiquitous bird on Swedish ponds, lakes, and slow rivers, recognisable by its jerky head-bobbing walk and distinctive red-and-yellow bill shield. Unlike coots, moorhens have lobed rather than fully webbed feet, which are used to grip reed stems as they clamber through vegetation. Moorhens are surprisingly aggressive during the breeding season, chasing rivals with vigorous foot-fights on the water surface. They build floating nests anchored to reeds and are among the earliest breeders in Swedish wetlands, sometimes nesting in late April while ice still lingers at the margins.

children.name: Gallinula chloropus
children.description: The rörhöna — a familiar sight on every Swedish pond and urban waterway, including Stockholm's city parks. Its bright red frontal shield and yellow-tipped bill are unmistakeable. Young birds from earlier broods help their parents raise later clutches — one of the few documented cases of cooperative breeding in Swedish waterbirds. Partial migrant in Sweden; most depart southward in autumn but some winter in mild lowland areas.

children.name: Gallinula galeata
children.description: The New World counterpart of the Common Moorhen, from which it was split following molecular and vocal studies. Found from southern Canada through Central and South America to the Falkland Islands, occupying similar wetland habitats to its Old World relative. The Common Gallinule is highly adaptable, thriving in urban parks, agricultural ponds, and mangrove edges, and is among the most frequently observed rallids across the Americas.

children.name: Gallinula tenebrosa
children.description: A large, dark moorhen of freshwater swamps, lakes, and slow rivers in Australia, New Guinea, and eastern Indonesia. Its red frontal shield, orange-red bill tip, and all-dark plumage distinguish it from the similar Common Moorhen. The Dusky Moorhen is highly social and forms loose groups outside the breeding season; it is one of the most commonly observed waterbirds on Australian wetlands and suburban water bodies.

children.name: Gallinula melanops
children.description: A handsome, small gallinule of freshwater marshes in south-eastern South America, distinguished by bold white spots on its dark flanks and a yellow-green frontal shield. It inhabits the same Pampas wetlands as several sympatric rallids but is more closely tied to dense emergent vegetation than open water. The Spot-flanked Gallinule is rarely common anywhere in its range and its population status warrants monitoring given ongoing wetland conversion in South America.

children.name: Gallinula angulata
children.description: Africa's smallest moorhen, found in freshwater marshes, flooded grasslands, and the margins of lakes and rivers across sub-Saharan Africa. Markedly smaller than the Common Moorhen, which it resembles in plumage pattern, the Lesser Moorhen is a largely nomadic species that follows rain and seasonal flooding to suitable wetlands. It is an intra-African migrant, moving north during the rains and south in the dry season, and can appear unpredictably in areas well outside its usual range.

children.name: Gallinula chloropus
children.description: A familiar waterbird of park ponds and marsh edges from Stockholm to southern Spain. Its red forehead shield, white undertail flashes, and habit of flicking its tail as it swims make it one of the most recognisable of all European marsh birds. In Sweden it nests in well-vegetated ponds and slow-moving waterways from Skåne to the central lake district, but its numbers decline in hard winters when smaller waters freeze. Their young are precocial and can swim within hours of hatching — they follow their parents through lily pads and reeds, often riding on a parent's back when the water gets too deep.

children.name: Gallinula tenebrosa
children.description: The Australian dusky moorhen is a common bird across eastern Australia, New Guinea, and Indonesia. It is slightly larger than the common moorhen and lacks the white flank streaks of its northern relative. Highly social and aggressive when breeding, forming large crèches of young tended by multiple adults. In the parks of Brisbane and Sydney it is one of the most conspicuous and familiar waterbirds, often approaching humans for food, a behaviour that has made it a beloved but sometimes overconfident suburban presence — particularly around the ornamental lakes of the Royal Botanic Garden where families of moorhens patrol the edges with a proprietorial air that endears them to generations of city-dwellers.

children.name: Gallinula melanops
children.description: A South American moorhen with distinctive white spots on the flanks. Found in marshes and reedbeds from Peru to Argentina.

children.name: Gallinula galeata
children.description: The New World equivalent of the common moorhen, formerly treated as conspecific. Distinguished by the shape of the red frontal shield and subtle differences in its vocalisations and breeding biology.

children.name: Gallinula sanctae
children.description: A moorhen of the Santa Cruz archipelago in the Galápagos Islands. Its isolated island population shows reduced flight capabilities.

children.name: Porphyrio
children.description: Swamphens and gallinules — large, often vibrantly coloured rallids with massive frontal shields and bills, long toes adapted for clambering through floating vegetation, and, in some species, extraordinary flight ability across oceanic distances. The genus spans the Old World tropics, Australasia, and the Americas, and includes the Takahē — one of the world's most famous conservation success stories. Swamphens are vocal, social birds that live in family groups and cooperatively defend territories.

children.name: Porphyrio porphyrio
children.description: A large, brilliantly blue-purple rallid of reedbeds, marsh edges, and vegetated lake shores across southern Europe, sub-Saharan Africa, and temperate Asia. Its massive red bill and frontal shield are unmistakeable. In Europe, the Purple Swamphen is largely confined to the Iberian Peninsula, southern France, and the Balkans, where it has recovered substantially following hunting pressure reductions. It walks with deliberate steps through floating vegetation, using its long toes to grip lily pads while feeding on plant stems and invertebrates.

children.name: Porphyrio melanotus
children.description: A large swamphen found across Australasia from Indonesia and the Philippines through New Guinea to Australia and New Zealand, where it is known as the Pūkeko. Gregarious and bold, it is one of the most conspicuous waterbirds of Australian and New Zealand wetlands, readily observed on roadsides and suburban parks near water. The Australasian Swamphen lives in cooperative breeding groups in which non-breeding helpers assist in raising chicks.

children.name: Porphyrio martinicus
children.description: One of the most spectacularly coloured rallids in the world, the Purple Gallinule combines iridescent blue-green and violet plumage with a red-and-yellow bill and powder-blue frontal shield. It breeds in freshwater marshes across the south-eastern United States and south through tropical America to northern Argentina. The Purple Gallinule is a remarkably strong flier and regularly turns up as a vagrant far north of its usual range, even in Europe.

children.name: Porphyrio alleni
children.description: A small, vividly coloured gallinule of freshwater wetlands across sub-Saharan Africa, distinguished by its turquoise frontal shield, iridescent green and purple plumage, and red bill and legs. An intra-African migrant, Allen's Gallinule follows seasonal rainfall to suitable wetlands and can appear as a vagrant far from its normal range. Despite its brilliant colours it is surprisingly secretive, often hiding in dense vegetation rather than walking in the open.

children.name: Porphyrio hochstetteri
children.description: A massive flightless gallinule endemic to New Zealand, famously declared extinct in 1898 but rediscovered in the remote Murchison Mountains of Fiordland in 1948 in one of conservation biology's most celebrated finds. Subsequent recovery efforts — including predator control, captive breeding, and island translocations — have raised the population from under 200 to over 500 individuals. The Takahē is now a symbol of New Zealand conservation success and a flagship for ecological restoration.

children.name: Fulica
children.description: Coots — robust, slate-black rallids with distinctive white bills and frontal shields that are familiar and often dominant birds on open Swedish lakes and reservoirs. The Eurasian coot (sothöna) is one of Sweden's most quarrelsome waterbirds: males engage in prolonged and sometimes violent territorial battles during the breeding season, rearing up from the water to kick and slash at rivals with their lobed feet. Coots are supremely adapted divers, using their large, individually lobed toes (rather than webbed feet) to propel themselves underwater in search of aquatic plants, which form the bulk of their diet. Their populations have expanded dramatically on Swedish lowland lakes since the 1970s, in part because nutrient-enriched waters support the dense aquatic vegetation they prefer.

children.name: Fulica atra
children.description: The sothöna — one of the most abundant and aggressive waterbirds in Sweden. Its all-black plumage with brilliant white bill and shield is distinctive at distance. Highly territorial; large lakes may host dozens of pairs separated by invisible but fiercely defended boundaries. Forms large winter flocks on unfrozen coastal bays and deep inland lakes. Nests of the sothöna — built from aquatic plants and anchored in shallow water — are among the most substantial of any Swedish bird.

children.name: Fulica americana
children.description: The most abundant coot in the Americas, found from Canada to Panama and wintering south to Colombia, with a separate population in the Caribbean. Unlike the Eurasian Coot, the American Coot has a distinctive dark reddish-brown frontal shield above the white bill. Enormous winter flocks gather on large lakes and estuaries, sometimes numbering tens of thousands of birds. American Coots are omnivorous and bold, frequently stealing food from diving ducks.

children.name: Fulica ardesiaca
children.description: A coot of high-altitude Andean lakes from Colombia south to Bolivia and Argentina, distinguished from the American Coot by its yellow-tipped bill and pale frontal shield. It inhabits highland bofedal wetlands and puna grassland lakes at elevations up to 4,500 metres. The Slate-coloured Coot often co-occurs with the Giant Coot on Andean lakes and is highly adapted to cold, oxygen-poor high-altitude environments.

children.name: Fulica armillata
children.description: A large coot of freshwater lakes, marshes, and slow rivers in south-eastern South America, from Brazil and Bolivia south to Tierra del Fuego. Its yellow bill with a distinctive red-orange band at the base is the key identification feature, which gives the species its common name. The Red-gartered Coot is highly gregarious outside the breeding season and forms large flocks on productive lowland lakes in Argentina, Uruguay, and southern Brazil.

children.name: Fulica cornuta
children.description: A remarkable high-Andean coot endemic to the altiplano of Bolivia, Chile, and Argentina, best known for its extraordinary nest — a massive cone of stones and plant material constructed on the surface of shallow lakes where no emergent vegetation is available. These stone mounds can weigh hundreds of kilograms and take years to build, representing one of the most impressive avian construction feats. The Horned Coot is classified as Vulnerable due to habitat degradation on Andean saline lakes.

children.name: Fulica cristata
children.description: A coot of freshwater lakes and marshes in sub-Saharan Africa and, historically, the Iberian Peninsula, where a small breeding population persists. Distinguished from the Eurasian Coot by two prominent red knobs at the top of the white frontal shield during the breeding season. The Iberian population is of significant conservation concern, having declined to only a few dozen breeding pairs in the late 20th century before partially recovering with active management.

children.name: Fulica gigantea
children.description: The world's largest coot, substantially bigger than the Eurasian Coot, inhabiting high-altitude saline and freshwater lakes on the Andean altiplano of Peru, Bolivia, Chile, and Argentina. Its massive size, yellow bill, and red frontal shield are distinctive. Giant Coots build enormous floating nests of aquatic vegetation anchored in shallow water, which can measure over a metre across. The species is classified as Near Threatened due to hunting and wetland degradation on Andean lakes.

children.name: Fulica atra
children.description: The familiar black waterbird with the white shield and bill — one of Sweden's most widespread and noisy marsh birds. Found on lakes and ponds from Skåne to Lapland, the Eurasian Coot is instantly recognisable by its aggressive behaviour during the breeding season, chasing off ducks, swans, and even its own young when they overstay their welcome. Its varied calls range from a loud, explosive 'kowk' to a sharp, repetitive 'pik-pik-pik' given in alarm and a higher-pitched, more rounded 'kitt' contact call between parents and chicks. Coots construct floating nests from dead reeds anchored to standing vegetation in shallow water, building them up as water levels rise, and the female lays clutches of 6–10 eggs that hatch over several days in an asynchrony that makes the starvation of the smallest chick a near-certainty in poor years. Despite their grumpy demeanour, their presence enlivens any Swedish lake.

children.name: Fulica americana
children.description: The American equivalent of the Eurasian Coot, differing in having a dark reddish-brown patch on the base of the white shield (more prominent at the start of the breeding season). Widely distributed across North America from Alaska and Hudson Bay to the highlands of Central America. Like its Eurasian relative, it is a notoriously aggressive defender of its floating nest, constructed from reeds and sedges in the shallow margins of freshwater lakes and ponds. American Coots are best known for the astonishing size and complexity of their family conflicts: females lay eggs not only in their own nests but also in the nests of neighbouring coots, creating an elaborate web of kinship and deception in the marsh community.

children.name: Fulica leucoptera
children.description: A common coot of southern South America, found in lakes and marshes from Brazil to Tierra del Fuego. Distinguished by white tips on the secondaries (visible in flight).

children.name: Fulica rufifrons
children.description: Named for its reddish-orange frontal shield (rather than white). Found in temperate marshes of southern Brazil, Uruguay, and Argentina.

children.name: Fulica cornuta
children.description: A high-Andean coot found on mountain lakes at altitudes of 3,000–5,000 m. Named for the two red knobs at the base of the shield. Builds a massive nest mound of stones in shallow water.

children.name: Fulica gigantea
children.description: The largest coot in the world, found at high elevations in the central Andes of Peru, Bolivia, and Chile. Its massive nest platforms, made of aquatic vegetation, are so large they create tiny floating islands used by other waterbirds after the breeding season.

children.name: Fulica armata
children.description: A coot of southern South America, with a distinctive red knob at the base of the bill and a bright yellow tip. Found from Brazil to the Falkland Islands.

children.name: Fulica leucoptera
children.description: A medium-sized coot with white tips on the secondary feathers, visible in flight. Common in freshwater marshes of the southern cone.

children.name: Fulica gigantea
children.description: The largest coot in the world, found at high elevations of the central Andes. Its massive nest platforms create small floating islands.

children.name: Pardirallus
children.description: Medium-sized rails of Central and South America with olive-brown upperparts and grey underparts.

children.name: Pardirallus sanguinolentus
children.description: A slate-grey rail of marshes from Peru to Chile and Argentina. Its long, greenish bill is distinctive. Feeds on snails, crustaceans, and insects.

children.name: Pardirallus rythirhynchus
children.description: Found in marshes of northern and central South America. Its blue-grey plumage and red bill with a white tip make it one of the most colourful members of the rail family.

children.name: Pardirallus nigricans
children.description: A dark, sooty-brown rail of the South American lowlands, from Colombia to Argentina. Its pale grey throat provides the only contrast in otherwise dark plumage. Found in marshes and wet grasslands.
